narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can take on—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it recognized in your profile. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the 2nd decile at your school, which stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward potential bar struggles, like a warning light that many similar students couldn't overcome. Right behind that was your 1st decile ranking from Year 1, reinforcing a pattern of early challenges that the model weighed heavily as a predictor of tougher times ahead on the exam. Your LSAT score of 24 added to that concern, as it fell short of the benchmarks where others typically powered through to success. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket painted a picture of fewer resources for the intense prep the bar demands, making this the fourth strongest factor tipping the scales toward failure in the model's view. Your background as a Black female applicant also aligned with historical trends the model flagged, not as a judgment, but as part of broader data patterns influencing outcomes. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9, while solid in effort, didn't counterbalance these, and even your full-time study commitment couldn't fully offset the combined weight. Together, these elements—especially those early and later grade deciles—created a 58% predicted chance of not passing the bar, which is why we had to make this tough call on your application. It's a snapshot of risks based on numbers, not your full potential or heart. Many in similar spots have turned things around with targeted boosts, like LSAT retakes or extra academic support—consider those as your next steps. You've got resilience in your story already; this is just one model's view, not your destiny. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
